scene_title: The same. Before the gates of Harfleur

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: How yet resolves the governor of the town?

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: This is the latest parle we will admit:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Therefore to our best mercy give yourselves;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Or like to men proud of destruction

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Defy us to our worst: for, as I am a soldier,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: A name that in my thoughts becomes me best,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: If I begin the battery once again,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I will not leave the half-achieved Harfleur

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Till in her ashes she lie buried.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: The gates of mercy shall be all shut up,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And the flesh’d soldier, rough and hard of heart,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: In liberty of bloody hand shall range

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: With conscience wide as hell, mowing like grass

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Your fresh-fair virgins and your flowering infants.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: What is it then to me, if impious War,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Array’d in flames like to the prince of fiends,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Do, with his smirch’d complexion, all fell feats

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Enlink’d to waste and desolation?

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: What is’t to me, when you yourselves are cause,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: If your pure maidens fall into the hand

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Of hot and forcing violation?

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: What rein can hold licentious wickedness

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: When down the hill he holds his fierce career?

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: We may as bootless spend our vain command

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Upon the enraged soldiers in their spoil

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: As send precepts to the leviathan

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: To come ashore. Therefore, you men of Harfleur,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Take pity of your town and of your people,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Whiles yet my soldiers are in my command;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Whiles yet the cool and temperate wind of grace

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: O’erblows the filthy and contagious clouds

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Of heady murder, spoil and villainy.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: If not, why, in a moment look to see

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: The blind and bloody soldier with foul hand

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Defile the locks of your shrill-shrieking daughters;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Your fathers taken by the silver beards,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And their most reverend heads dash’d to the walls,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Your naked infants spitted upon pikes,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Whiles the mad mothers with their howls confused

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Do break the clouds, as did the wives of Jewry

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: At Herod’s bloody-hunting slaughtermen.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: What say you? will you yield, and this avoid,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Or, guilty in defence, be thus destroy’d?

lines.speaker: GOVERNOR
lines.text: Our expectation hath this day an end:

lines.speaker: GOVERNOR
lines.text: The Dauphin, whom of succours we entreated,

lines.speaker: GOVERNOR
lines.text: Returns us that his powers are yet not ready

lines.speaker: GOVERNOR
lines.text: To raise so great a siege. Therefore, great king,

lines.speaker: GOVERNOR
lines.text: We yield our town and lives to thy soft mercy.

lines.speaker: GOVERNOR
lines.text: Enter our gates; dispose of us and ours;

lines.speaker: GOVERNOR
lines.text: For we no longer are defensible.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Open your gates. Come, uncle Exeter,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Go you and enter Harfleur; there remain,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And fortify it strongly ’gainst the French:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Use mercy to them all. For us, dear uncle,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: The winter coming on and sickness growing

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Upon our soldiers, we will retire to Calais.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Tonight in Harfleur will we be your guest;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Tomorrow for the march are we addrest.

stage_directions.text: The Governor and some Citizens on the walls; the English forces below. Enter King Henry and his train.
Flourish. The King and his train enter the town.

glosses.definition: decides; answers
the town's military commander, empowered to surrender
last parley — the final chance to negotiate before assault

glosses.phrase: to our best mercy give yourselves
glosses.definition: surrender unconditionally to our mercy
prideful in wanting their own destruction
artillery bombardment
half-taken; half-conquered

glosses.phrase: in her ashes she lie buried
glosses.definition: until the town is burnt and buried in its own ashes

glosses.phrase: gates of mercy shall be all shut up
glosses.definition: mercy will be withdrawn — the speech's opening premise
soldier accustomed to killing — 'flesh'd' = blooded, habituated to slaughter
free licence to kill — the abandonment of military restraint
a conscience as vast and permissive as hell itself
cutting down as a scythe cuts grass — slaughter as harvest

glosses.phrase: fresh-fair virgins and your flowering infants
glosses.definition: the young women and small children — named explicitly as targets
war personified as unholy — 'impious' = sacrilegious
Satan
soot-blackened face — war imagined as a fire-blackened demon
cruel deeds — 'fell' = savage, deadly
linked to destruction and ruin
chaste young women
heated, forcible rape — the threat named directly
lawless evil — soldiers' unchained lust and rage
runs his headlong course — 'career' = gallop
pointless; useless
commands to the great sea-monster — Henry's image for impossibility

glosses.phrase: Whiles yet my soldiers are in my command
glosses.definition: while I can still control them — the threat's pivot: after a certain point, he cannot restrain them

glosses.phrase: cool and temperate wind of grace
glosses.definition: the mild breeze of mercy
blows away
impulsive, headlong killing
the soldier who kills indiscriminately

glosses.phrase: Defile the locks of your shrill-shrieking daughters
glosses.definition: rape your daughters — 'defile the locks' = tear at their hair as they are assaulted
the grey beards of the old men — dragged by the elders

glosses.phrase: reverend heads dash’d to the walls
glosses.definition: old men's venerable heads smashed against the walls
babies impaled on long pole-weapons — the speech's most infamous image, left unambiguous on the page
the women of Judea — biblical reference to Matthew 2, Herod's massacre of the infants at Bethlehem
Herod's soldiers hunting babies — the Massacre of the Innocents, the benchmark Henry's threat invokes
responsible, by defending the town, for what follows
reinforcements; relief
the Dauphin's army is not yet mustered
break so large a siege
Harfleur's formal surrender
able to hold out
garrison and reinforce the captured town
spare the citizens — Henry pivots instantly from threat to mercy, having achieved surrender

glosses.phrase: winter coming on and sickness growing
glosses.definition: dysentery ravaged the English army after the siege — Henry's march to Calais is forced by attrition
withdraw to the English-held port, ~100 miles north
ready; prepared (archaic)

annotations.title: The threat to Harfleur — what is actually on the page
annotations.body: Henry's speech to the governor of Harfleur is the play's hardest passage. The Chorus does not prepare us for it; the action scene does not euphemize it. In order, Henry threatens: the enraged soldiery in 'liberty of bloody hand,' with 'conscience wide as hell,' mowing virgins and infants like grass (lines 11–14); fire and desolation (15–18); the 'hot and forcing violation' of pure maidens — that is, the mass rape of the town's women (19–21); the defiling of 'shrill-shrieking daughters' and the dashing of old men's heads against the walls (34–37); and, most unmistakably, 'Your naked infants spitted upon pikes' (38). Henry compares the coming scene to Herod's massacre of the innocents at Bethlehem (40–41), thus locating his threat inside the Christian catalogue of atrocities. This is not a bluff; it is the standard early-modern rhetoric of siege warfare, in which credible threats of sack were conventional tools for forcing surrender. But Shakespeare's version is unusually specific and unusually sustained. Forty-three lines of threat, naming sexual violence and infanticide explicitly. Readers cannot honestly say Henry is a simple hero and hold these lines in mind at the same time. The play makes us hold both.
sources: Theodor Meron, Bloody Constraint: War and Chivalry in Shakespeare (1998)
Norman Rabkin, 'Either/Or: Responding to Henry V,' Shakespeare and the Problem of Meaning (1981)
Juliet Barker, Agincourt: The King, the Campaign, the Battle (2005)

annotations.title: Henry's pivot from threat to mercy
annotations.body: The governor surrenders, and Henry pivots instantly: 'Use mercy to them all' (line 53). The same man who forty lines earlier was promising pikes through infants is now commanding his uncle Exeter to garrison Harfleur gently. The speed of the pivot is the point. Henry's threat worked: the town yielded 'our town and lives to thy soft mercy' rather than endure sack. The play thus invites two readings simultaneously. (1) Henry is a cunning military psychologist who knows the conventions of siege warfare: make the threat credible enough and the city surrenders, sparing both sides. No rape happens; no infant is spitted. The threat is instrumental. (2) Henry is a rhetorician who can summon the imagery of atrocity without hesitation and withdraw it without explanation; what matters is what he was willing to promise. The scene refuses to decide. The structural fact — threat, surrender, mercy — is historically accurate and morally unresolved.

annotations.title: The march to Calais — forced by dysentery
annotations.body: Henry's closing announcement — 'The winter coming on and sickness growing / Upon our soldiers, we will retire to Calais' — corresponds to a historical fact the play slightly softens. The English army was ravaged by dysentery during and after the siege of Harfleur; thousands of men died of the 'bloody flux'; Henry's best estimate of his effective force by the time of Agincourt was a fraction of what had landed. The march to Calais was not a strategic decision but a desperate one, undertaken with depleted forces through hostile territory. Historically this is the situation that made the victory at Agincourt so improbable and so mythologized afterwards. The play will register the depletion (Henry to Montjoy in 3.6: 'My people are with sickness much enfeebled'), but it presents the retirement to Calais as a poised royal choice rather than an epidemiological emergency.
sources: Anne Curry, Agincourt: A New History (2005)
Juliet Barker, Agincourt: The King, the Campaign, the Battle (2005)

trials.prompt: What, specifically, does Henry threaten if Harfleur does not surrender?
options: The mass rape of the town's women, the killing of its old men, and the impaling of its 'naked infants' on pikes — with an explicit comparison to Herod's massacre of the innocents
The removal of the town's governor

trials.prompt: How should readers think about Henry's threat?
options: As a pure expression of villainy

As the standard early-modern rhetoric of siege warfare, in which credible threats were conventional surrender-tools — but Shakespeare's version is unusually specific and sustained, naming sexual violence and infanticide; the moral weight is on the page and is not euphemized
As a rhetorical flourish meant to impress the soldiers
As a passage unique to the Folio and cut from modern editions

trials.prompt: What happens immediately after Henry's threat?
options: The town surrenders, and Henry pivots instantly to 'Use mercy to them all' — the scene stages threat, surrender, and sudden mercy in forty lines
Henry withdraws without taking the town

trials.prompt: Why does the scene end with Henry's decision to march to Calais?
options: Because he has defeated the French entirely
The army has been ravaged by dysentery during the siege; the retreat is historically forced by disease, though the play presents it as a poised royal choice
Because he wants to visit English garrisons
Because Montjoy has offered a truce
